RT @ejfoxverbose: Just read "TEDxStanford -Tina Seelig - A crash course in creativity - YouTube" http://t.co/W8fiK8Ux (via @pocket)

@ivarvong @simonw Imo its more about closeness and centrality, in the center of your sentences you usually find your higher-level embedding – the smaller ones orbit around it - you could probably make a tree out of it but thats not how my brain thinks about it right now
@ivarvong @simonw Yes, exactly - then the hard part is aggregation and visualizing the aggregations in an intuitive way- my favorite type of problem

@simonw This also allows you some control on how much you “zoom out” when aggregating together related embeddings- the smaller the better lets you find links between clusters and pull a bit from each, or the entire cluster, whatever suits my task
@simonw Ah, I am always clustering / grouping my embeddings- so the smaller the better leads me to the correct cluster, and then that entire cluster is used to inform the rest of the process
@simonw Imo by logical sub-grouping of the data (not random/line-based) if code, then embed each method, if text, each sentence or paragraph, etc- the smaller the better, cost allowing
